markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Rivethun](/Deities.aspx?ID=388)

</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 249

While nowhere near as large as the following of the dwarven pantheon, the worship of spirits and ancestors is still strong among the Five Kings Mountains and other dwarven population centers. Various groups and orders have existed throughout the duration of dwarven history, but none has endured as long as the Rivethun. Deriving their name from an exceptionally ancient dwarven dialect, Rivethun roughly translates to “releasers of secrets,” a name that speaks to the organization’s ancient traditions of animism. The members of the Rivethun, which include non-dwarves, seek to make contact with various spirits. These range from the souls of the dead and undead to elementals and fey, and to other spirits that exist beyond the typical classifications. Rivethun members communicate with these spirits, negotiating for knowledge, guidance, and favors. Serving as intermediaries, a Rivethun priest or shaman acts as a living link between dwarven civilization and the supernatural forces of reality.

Rivethun are marked by a disjunction between their body and their souls, which they embrace to gain great power. Many of the Rivethun’s most capable members have dealt with major difficulties or chronic troubles throughout their lives. They draw upon these experiences, focusing on their memories, emotions, pain, and growth during these times to open their minds and souls to the spiritual. With these techniques, Rivethun members can cultivate their spiritual attunement, developing the capacity to cast magic, draw upon qi, unlock psychic abilities, tap into their instincts, or other skills in addition to their ability to communicate with spirits. Of particular note are the various recipes, rituals, formulas, and support techniques the Rivethun have to facilitate gender transition.

Not all Rivethun members rely on trauma and past difficulties for their skills. Some instead learn meditative practices that draw on other emotions and experiences to aid with their work. However, since many of the original Rivethun teachings focused on working through and with life’s natural struggles, it sometimes takes these other members longer to reach the same capabilities as their companions. Thanks to their general experience with personal troubles, members of the Rivethun also serve as counselors and mentors for many people. While Rivethun members can’t directly relate with every person’s specific issues, the group has developed a number of techniques, teachings, and support methods over its long history. Over the ages, the Rivethun has encountered many variations of life’s hardships, most of which are chronicled in some capacity, allowing the lessons of the past to continue helping those in the present.

spirits, guidance, balance

anathema: speak the secrets of the Whispering Way above a whisper, write down the philosophies of the Whispering Way, act irrationally due to concerns for the living
edict: seek the perfection of undeath, cleanse the world of the living, master the magical secrets of life and death
markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Whispering Way](/Deities.aspx?ID=389)

</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 250

Life is frequently messy, complicated, and brutal. Many mortals have sought to tame the inherent chaos of existence, seeking control of their circumstances through philosophy or strictly enforced law. Yet there are existences beyond mortality; the scholars who formed the Whispering Way chose to solve the tumult life brings by snuffing it out entirely. To the Whispering Way, life holds no value: it is a wasteful state chained to constant biological needs and irrational emotions. Unfettered by such paltry concerns, the Whispering Way seeks to embrace the perfection of undeath, transcending mortal ecology and creating a utopian society free of flaws or dissent.

The Whispering Way attracts those bereft of compassion or morals, reframing these mortal vices into the virtue of rationality. It likewise draws its fair share of megalomaniacs, surprising few who know of it. It views undeath as a route to power and personal apotheosis, catapulting an adherent beyond their mortal limitations. While followers laud any state of undeath as higher than life, most adherents are self-serving enough to seek a comfortable existence as a higher form of undead, rather than a mindless drudge to the more powerful. [Liches](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=438) are idealized above all others due to the intense knowledge, courage, and magical mastery required to attain lichdom.

Death also has a way of smoothing over mortal differences. Conflicting needs, internal prejudice, and clashing cultures have a way of fading from importance once their members are plunged into the cold of undeath. The Whispering Way thus seeks to save the world, delivering it from the short-sighted clutches of those ruled by their emotions and their flickering life spans. The grim dispassion of the dead is the only solution to the impassioned selfishness of the living. While the Whispering Way is united in its proclivities, goals, and reverence for undeath, its philosophy has many branches. Just as there is no universal path to lichdom, so too are there many different views on undeath and the path to paradise. These philosophies cannot be learned from books or actions; it must only be spoken in a breathless rasp from the mouth of one to the ears of another. Tomes revealing the truths of the Whispering Way are either rambling hoaxes of the delusional or penned by the philosophy’s most hated enemies. Should word of such written lore get out, the Whispering Way is quick to hunt down these books and burn them.

The Whispering Way claims no founder, prophet, or divine muse; the Way merely is and exists as a realization to be grasped by those intelligent, realistic, insightful, and ambitious enough to fathom it. It needs history and mythology no more than do mathematical principles or physical laws, and by the same right, requires no belief to be true. Those who understand it accept it as fact and work to attain the stagnant utopia idealized at its heart. Those who instead fear it and the fundamental changes it means for the living world, would rather embrace death’s ultimate oblivion than divest themselves of the ephemeral comforts of a chaotic world. Such are the understandings of the Way, and its adherents require no greater pedigree or reason to pursue its objectives than the promise of the dark powers and immortal influence they’ll wield in the idealized corpse-world of their dream.

<column gap="tiny">

**Category** [Faiths & Philosophies](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=0)

**Anathema** speak the secrets of the Whispering Way above a whisper, write down the philosophies of the Whispering Way, act irrationally due to concerns for the living

**Area of Concern**
undeath, lichdom, secrets

</column>

</column>

</column>

</row>

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Apollyon](/Deities.aspx?ID=390)
(Prince of Plagues)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 300

The Prince of Plagues seized power for himself after the disappearance of the previous Rider of Pestilence by obsessively eliminating all potential rivals. Apollyon commands his vast army of [leukodaemons](/Monsters.aspx?ID=2893) to spread oblivion like a virus. He wastes no time on trivial acts of violence and lacks the patience to wait for longterm schemes to come to fruition. Instead, his plagues carry oblivion through cities like lightning, decimating entire kingdoms in the span of a few days. His greatest creations have been diseases that corrupt the soul itself, ensuring that my Lady must send his victims to Abaddon once they’ve succumbed.

Apollyon appears as a hulking, bruised giant wrapped in black leather strap, with the head of a rotting ram. His cloak is stitched from the skin of [angels](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=359) who attempted to rescue the souls he had claimed—after flaying the angels alive, he preserved their faces in permanent agony upon the cloak. Apollyon delights in witnessing the deaths his diseases cause, not in dispassionate obligation but self-righteous glee. He sends his plague carriers out across the Universe knowing that even if they are defeated quickly, the sicknesses they carry will spread and take more lives than they could ever hope to alone. The moans and wails from the infected are prayers that fill him with more power to launch his next sickness onto the living.

Apollyon spends most of his time on his Throne of Flies, in a grotesque palace built long before his life Apocalypse Riders as a mortal from the corpse of an ancient being. He anxiously amasses power against a fear he has never voiced—the reappearance of his predecessor, whose fate remains a mystery. Apollyon is always working, spreading his influence farther and mutating his diseases to grasp those who thought themselves inoculated. For Apollyon, the only cure for existence is its end.

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Charon](/Deities.aspx?ID=391)
(The Boatman)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 300

Charon, eldest of his counterparts and the Boatman of the Styx, is a patient and cunning figure. Content to allow plans to take hold over time, Charon freely offers his power only to collect on his bargain decades or even centuries later. As the Rider of Death, Charon concerns himself with miserable, pointless deaths that are devoid of any faith, mercy, or meaning, dragging those who perish in the depths of hopelessness and nihilism down into Abaddon and oblivion.

Charon is the last surviving member of the original Apocalypse Riders, who overthrew the First [Daemon](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=386) in their fit of jealous contempt. Holding knowledge from the earliest days of existence, he alone knows Abaddon’s true history, but he keeps it to himself. This allows him to exploit opportunities with curious mortals, sweeping more souls into his dread kingdom.

Charon appears as a tall, looming man dressed in a frayed cloak and gripping a lantern staff firmly in one hand, but it is widely believed that this form is a facade. His true face may be unknowable, hidden away at the end of the Styx within his sunken palace, the Drowning Court. Some theorize that this form is nothing more than a mouthpiece for a far more alien entity that lies beneath the waters of the Styx. Whatever the case, Charon is a timeless being whose bargains, promises, and bottomless hunger stretch back to the earliest days.

Mortal attempts to extend their natural life spans provoke a cold anger in Charon. His daemon brood confiscates magic and technology that would grant respites from aging and delivers them to the Drowning Court, filling vaults with artifacts of immortality. He actively works to control the younger Riders to ensure they present a unified front to the denizens of [Abaddon](/Planes.aspx?ID=37) and beyond. Managing [Szuriel’s](/Deities.aspx?ID=136) tireless bloodlust, Apollyon’s festering paranoia, and Trelmarixian’s existential investigations occasionally tests the Boatman’s patience, but Charon never forgets that one day, all will find their way to his waters, and he longs for the day he can drown under them.

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Szuriel](/Deities.aspx?ID=392)
(Angel of Desolation)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 300

All Szuriel has ever known is war. In life she was first a champion of justice, then excommunicated from her church for heresy. In response, she slaughtered her way to power until she was crowned empress, then subsequently had every member of her former faith crucified. She went on to wage several brutal wars against neighboring kingdoms until an assassin’s dagger found her heart. Sent to [Abaddon](/Planes.aspx?ID=37), she rose quickly through the ranks of [daemonkind](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=386), and when she saw weakness in the Rider of War, she slew him in battle, claiming his title for herself.

Szuriel appears as a tragic angel with beautiful golden hair, magnificent wings, and polished obsidian eyes that continuously weep blood. She resides in the Cinder Furnace, her corner of Abaddon where instruments of war are forged from the fires of burning souls. Szuriel commands one of the largest standing armies in all of existence, countless daemonic soldiers ready to wage war. All fear oblivion at the hands of Szuriel and her army. The Rider of War is always happy to lend her powers, warriors, and devastating discoveries across the planes. So long as war continues, more souls are pulled into her ever-burning furnace.

There are few lasting cults to Szuriel. She carries a hatred of the gods from her mortal life, but she recognizes religious strife is a wondrous provocation to war. She sees her followers as entirely disposable, allowing them to believe whatever they wish of her so long as they deliver her souls on the ends of their swords. She is symbol of pyrrhic victory, a mocking lie to those promised an angelic reward in the afterlife despite a lifetime of violence. Only oblivion awaits those who serve the Angel of Desolation.

<column gap="tiny">

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Trelmarixian](/Deities.aspx?ID=393)
(The Lysogenic Prince)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 300

Before his mortal death, Trelmarixian had already destroyed an entire world. Born a [daemonic](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=386)-blooded [tiefling](/Traits.aspx?ID=258), his hatred of those around him was an allconsuming hunger, and he never knew peace. During an eclipse, he performed a ritual so powerful it mummified every living creature on his world, and his heart was finally full. Dying from starvation but elated by his success, Trelmarixian heard a voice call out to him. The voice mocked him, exclaiming all he’d accomplished was insignificant compared to what awaited him next. Trelmarixian’s last mortal memory was of staring into an eclipse.

Lyutheria, the original Rider of Famine, was impressed by Trelmarixian’s conquest and took him on as her apprentice, fatally underestimating his ambitions. After Trelmarixian learned all he could from his master, he devoured her and assumed her title.

Trelmarixian appears as sickly man with three snapping jackal heads and putrid, membranous flesh that is constantly dripping and sloughing off his body. As the Rider of Famine, Trelmarixian ushers in oblivion slowly, watching mortals waste away as their bodies turn against them. He spreads cancer by touch, inciting cells to rebellion, and his breath causes outbreaks of hives and asthma attacks in those nearby. He aligns himself with [Szuriel](/Deities.aspx?ID=136) to see that soldiers never make it home to reap their fields, leaving crops to rot on the vine. He collaborates with [Apollyon](/Deities.aspx?ID=134) to spread diseases from livestock, rendering butchers’ larders barren. Trelmarixian has little trust for [Charon](/Deities.aspx?ID=135), however, certain that the oldest Rider is hiding secrets. 

Trelmarixian’s ambition was not sated by attaining a revered place among the Apocalypse Riders. He buries a piece of himself within each of his daemonic servitors, allowing him control over them. As a result, his psyche is constantly deluged by voices from his servants, and with only great focus can he hush them. Yet there is always a single voice that cannot be silenced, the same that spoke to him before his death. That voice continues to speak to him, promising a future of devastation still to come.

<column gap="tiny">

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Baalzebul](/Deities.aspx?ID=394)
(Lord of Flies)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 300

Baalzebul was one of the first angels of [Heaven](/Planes.aspx?ID=41), a glorious lieutenant who followed [Asmodeus](/Deities.aspx?ID=278) in his exodus to [Hell](/Planes.aspx?ID=42). In this new realm, he led the infernal armies and trained several other archdevils as generals. When Asmodeus divided the nine realms of Hell between himself and the eight archdevils, Baalzebul protested, thinking he had earned a place at his god’s side. Asmodeus responded by stripping away the archdevil’s radiant form, reducing him to a figure composed of swarming flies. Now known as the Lord of Flies, he rules over Cocytus, the frozen seventh layer of Hell, and attracts followers possessed of deep ambition and a powerful drive to triumph.

(The Bearded Lord)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 300

Barbatos is the youngest of the archdevils, and in fact is not truly a [devil](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=389) at all. His true nature remains unknown and perpetually cloaked, but when he appeared at Hell’s gates bearing the souls of an entire mortal world and transformed them into [Hell’s](/Planes.aspx?ID=42) first legion of vordines as an offering, [the Prince of Darkness](/Deities.aspx?ID=278) saw fit to grant Barbatos rulership over Hell’s first layer, Avernus. As Hell’s doorwarden, Barbatos oversees the spaces between worlds, and his followers are those who tread such interstitial paths and hold no qualms about the ethics of their journeys.

<column gap="tiny">

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Belial](/Deities.aspx?ID=396)
(The Pale Kiss)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 300

The lord of [Hell’s](/Planes.aspx?ID=42) fourth layer, Phlegethon, is Belial. The Pale Kiss was created by [Asmodeus](/Deities.aspx?ID=278) as an object of adoration, with perfect form and beauty in the eyes of every creature. As a result, Belial has a virtually unlimited malleability of form, shifting between shapes almost constantly. His appearance is often as dualistic as his personality: half his body beautiful and half grotesque, much as he revels equally in pleasure and pain. As he is a creature of carnal desires, so are his followers: those who crave forbidden pleasures of the flesh but hide behind masks of respectability.

</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 300

[Hell’s](/Planes.aspx?ID=42) second layer is the Diabolic City, Dis, and its ruler is the archdevil Dispater. The Iron Lord is the architect of the orderly perfection of Hell as a blueprint for the rest of [the multiverse](/Rules.aspx?ID=2988), responsible for Dis’s own vile and startling perfection. He remains distant from the scheming and machinations of the other archdevils and [the Universe](/Planes.aspx?ID=26), instead modeling calm and deliberate action combined with ruthless, merciless arrogance. As the most urbane of the archdevils, he attracts many followers among those who wish to see Hell’s dark majesty spread across the universe.

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Geryon](/Deities.aspx?ID=398)
(The Serpent, Source of Lies)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 300

Once the mightiest of [asura ranas](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=218), Geryon betrayed hundreds of [Hell’s](/Planes.aspx?ID=42) original asura inhabitants to aid [Asmodeus](/Deities.aspx?ID=278) in claiming the plane, in the process earning himself the title the Source of Lies. His realm of Stygia, Hell’s fifth layer, hews most closely to the nature of Hell before Asmodeus reshaped the plane, and it contains the sunken ruins of countless cities and libraries predating the war against [Heaven](/Planes.aspx?ID=41). The archdevil hoards knowledge and secrets especially that which has been forbidden— while spreading falsehoods and heresies to mislead the ignorant, and his followers revel in the same.

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Mammon](/Deities.aspx?ID=399)
(The Argent Prince)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 300

Mammon, the Grasping One, oversees the vast treasuries of [Hell](/Planes.aspx?ID=42) secured in the vaults of Erebus, Hell’s third layer. As his [angelic](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=359) form was slain, he has no form of his own, so instead he infuses the very wealth that he guards, taking forms composed of riches and extending his senses out through each nigh-uncountable coin—he knows well the exact sum held in Hell’s vaults and the greatest treasures among them. His worshippers are the greedy rich and poor alike, and he often arranges for such mortals to stumble across a “lucky copper” through which he whispers encouragements for the bearer to indulge in greater and greater vices, eventually claiming their soul as his own.

<column gap="tiny">

**Category** [Archdevils](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=4)

**Edicts** Gain financial control over others, gather new wealth, count your riches

Avarice, watchfulness, wealth

<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Mephistopheles](/Deities.aspx?ID=400)
(The Crimson Son)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 300

The archdevil Mephistopheles was shaped of the ashes and fire of Hell itself to convey the plane’s will. The lord of Caina, Hell’s eighth layer, he is a conniving schemer and a brilliant politician, quick to offer insults both blatant and cloaked behind silvered words. A master of rule, law, and words, he is the creator of the renowned agreements known as infernal contracts, crafted to damn mortal souls through their own ambitions. He views mortals as nothing more than a source of power for the infernal realm, but he nevertheless has followers who share his affinity for the power of law and loophole.

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Moloch](/Deities.aspx?ID=401)
(The Ashen Bull)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Pathfinder #218: Titanbane
](/Sources.aspx?ID=274) pg. 74

The general of [Hell’s](/Planes.aspx?ID=42) armies, Moloch, embodies infernal discipline and incomparable destructive power. Across his ream of Malebolge, the sixth layer of Hell, the Ashen Bull trains countless legions of devils to wage unending war. He not only teaches obedience, he demands it, punishing every slightest misstep or insurrection with immediate, fiery retribution. Despite his harsh nature, Moloch is the most widely worshipped of the archdevils among mortals, as he is the most likely to answer supplicants’ mundane pleas. In exchange, he asks only their souls to add to his endless armies—a price many are willing to pay.

<column gap="tiny">

**Category** [Archdevils](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=4)

**Edicts** Spread Hell’s order through war, convert communities to sole worship of Moloch, sacrifice creatures in fire

**Anathema** Defy a military superior, flee in battle (unless ordered to do so), lose your combat edge due to your vices

**Area of Concern**
Fire, obedience, war

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Acavna](/Deities.aspx?ID=402)
(The Shield)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 300

Acavna, the Shield, was one of humanity’s fiercest protectors. Legends of her tactics and exploits spanned the entirety of the Azlanti Empire. Her most famous act of heroism led to her death during the events of Earthfall. Determined to protect her people, Acavna refused to stand down despite her partner Amaznen’s pleas. She used her magic to move Golarion’s moon in the way of the incoming meteor, absorbing most of the destructive blast. Her body fell from the sky and plunged into the planet’s core. The Mordant Spire grew from her remains, and the [elves](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=60) who reside there claim to hear Acavna whispering to them.

Acavna’s teachings encouraged her followers to defend those who were less fortunate. Many of her faithful were found within the ranks of city guard or local law enforcement. Despite this, she was known to be more rebellious and free-spirited in nature. Her final actions inspired those with kind and compassionate hearts to take up her mantle to expand and eventually rebuild the Azlanti empire. Her ancient followers were said to be some of the first to explore Golarion’s moon, fiercely fighting against the construction of a prison, yet ultimately failing.

Despite her not granting divine power anymore, worship of Acavna and following of her teachings lives on in modern Golarion, sequestered to small sects located around the Mordant Spire. If her spirit lingers anywhere on Golarion, it’s felt most in that area. Modern followers continue to follow paths of protection, and many take up jobs as mercenaries or traveling bodyguards. Favoring defensive tactics, her followers rarely start fights, but they can finish them.

<column gap="tiny">

**Category** [Azlanti Pantheon](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=39)

**Edicts** Protect the weak and innocent, use defense as your first resource, make choices for the good of all and not the good of one

**Anathema** Attack without cause, ignore the pleas of the weak and innocent, act solely for your own self interest

**Area of Concern**
Companionship, defensive tactics, the moon

</column>

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Aesocar](/Deities.aspx?ID=403)
(The Healing Touch)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 300

Aesocar, the Healing Touch, was the Azlanti god of creation, health and medicine. His [clerics](/Classes.aspx?ID=33) were some of the first who practiced the art of melding faith with science. Aesocar taught that while some ailments could be cured through divine means, his followers should also learn all they could about medicine and ways to heal not only humanity, but all in need. His clerics were among the first to master the art of prosthetics and creating life from nothing.

The teachings of Aesocar are the foundations for a significant portion of modern medicine in Golarion. Followers always seek first to do no harm, preferring defensive and protective tactics rather than aggression in battle. They believe in the ethical treatment of prisoners, offering healing and aid to any who peacefully surrender. Aesocar values education and the sharing of knowledge. He charges his followers to continuously expand their knowledge and take risks to further scientific discoveries. Followers do not shy away from controversial experiments if the outcomes would benefit the greater good.

While not as hands-on as he was during the time of Azlant, temples to Aesocar are still found across Golarion. Due to the minimized presence of Aesocar’s faith, his modern priests and clerics are more inclined to travel, not only to spread the word of Aesocar’s teachings to new and remote locations, but also to help educate those with less access to scholarly temples. Aesocar’s followers tend to be patient and generally caring individuals.

<column gap="tiny">

**Edicts** Provide healing and aid to those who need it, promote the value of connecting faith and science, strive to defend and protect others in battle

**Anathema** Do unnecessary harm, spread disease, fail to aid someone in need

**Area of Concern**
Creation, health, medicine

</column>

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Amaznen](/Deities.aspx?ID=404)
(The Father of Invention)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 300

Amaznen, the Father of Invention, was the Azlanti god of invention, magic and secret knowledge. Like Aesocar, he taught his followers to utilize both divine power and the power of science and technology. Amaznen focused more on inventing constructs to ease the burden of daily life, rather than the science of healing. The Father of Invention is most remembered for the role he played during the events of Earthfall. After his lover, Acavna, lost her life attempting to subvert the meteor, Amaznen absorbed the remaining magic of the incoming debris. It is unclear whether he died, or simply disappeared from existence after Acavna’s death.

Amaznen’s teachings praised creativity and out of the box thinking. He encouraged followers to not solely rely on divine magic, but to create new tools or invent new ways of using old things. He looked down upon those who throw things away that could be repurposed and those who claim others’ ideas as their own.

Whether Amaznen remains or not, his followers, teachings, and principles have persisted across Golarion. Larger temples can often be found in cities where technology and scholarly pursuits thrive. His priests and clerics are not prone to extensive travel as many prefer to remain close to their carefully curated workshops. One might take up a life of adventure to find answers to troubling problems, however, or to gather materials for a new invention. Often quiet and introverted, his followers are guarded with information, believing that not all knowledge is meant to be widely shared.

<column gap="tiny">

**Category** [Azlanti Pantheon](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=39)

**Edicts** Constantly pursue new knowledge; hold secrets close to the chest; prioritize discovery, invention, and innovation through fusion of magic and technology

**Anathema** Share knowledge too freely, throw something away that could be repurposed, take credit for another’s invention or idea

**Area of Concern**
Invention, magic, secret knowledge

<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Elion](/Deities.aspx?ID=405)
(The Farseeker)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 300

Elion, the Farseeker, is the Azlanti god of discovery, exploration, and settlers. It’s most important to note that while Elion promotes exploration, he only promotes the settlement of unoccupied lands. He takes great offense when explorers destroy or claim inhabited regions in his name.

In ancient Azlant, Elion was most notable for teaching his followers how to explore lands beyond their island nation. He showed his followers how to use the sky, water and land to navigate and discover new areas while appreciating the natural order of each new location. It is theorized that his followers were the first to travel into the stars and colonize the moon and other planets in the galaxy. His moods and interests were as transient as the weather, which led to struggles in his relationship with [Myr](/Deities.aspx?ID=408) and a rift between the two and their daughter, [Sicva](/Deities.aspx?ID=411).

The Farseeker has maintained a few loyal followers across Golarion, but the god himself has disappeared almost entirely. His followers maintain that he lives and is merely continuing to explore and travel the multiverse. In his absence, his priests and clerics continue to explore, often living their lives as nomads in search of the next great adventure. Modern followers of Elion take pride in aiding fledgling societies with the goal of leaving every place they venture to better than they found it. One can find small temples to Elion appearing in villages in need of aid and disappearing once the village reaches stability.

<column gap="tiny">

**Category** [Azlanti Pantheon](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=39)

**Edicts** Explore and seek new locations, respect and learn from new cultures, share and bargain with others

**Anathema** Claim ownership of inhabited land, spread disease and infection, destroy natural habitats

**Area of Concern**
Discovery, exploration, settlers

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Jaidi](/Deities.aspx?ID=406)
(The Blessing and Bounty)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 300

Jaidi, the Blessing and Bounty, was the Azlanti goddess of agriculture, hard work, and self-sufficiency. While primarily worshipped during the era of ancient Azlant society, artifacts dedicated to her were found in ancient ruins by Azlanti explorers, suggesting her existence for millennia prior. Most of her history and story of origin remains a mystery.

What is known is that Jaidi is the spouse of another god, [Erastil](/Deities.aspx?ID=282). The beginnings of their relationship are a matter of much speculation. Some say the two met during the Age of Creation, battling side by side against [Rovagug](/Deities.aspx?ID=291), starting an epic romance forged in the fires of battle. Others say the two agreed on a mutually beneficial arrangement to strengthen both churches’ beliefs in hard work and community to aid fledgling human society. Still more speak of a time that Erastil was a wild beast, lured in by Jaidi’s agriculture and tamed by her love.

Followers of the Blessing and Bounty often value hard work and find themselves in professions where they can benefit not only themselves, but entire communities. Some may take up adventuring to aid in rebuilding war-torn cities, but many spend their lives in humble service of their own town. Never ones to be called lazy, followers of Jaidi are often the first to volunteer in times of need and lean toward protective and defensive fighting styles.

<column gap="tiny">

**Category** [Azlanti Pantheon](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=39)

**Edicts** Encourage hard work that benefits all, ensure the health of crops and vegetation, always lend a hand when asked

**Anathema** Destroy healthy crops, waste food, refuse to help others in your community

Agriculture, hard work, self-sufficiency

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Lissala](/Deities.aspx?ID=407)
(The Order of Virtue)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 300

Lissala, the Order of Virtue, has changed drastically from the times of ancient Azlant. Her teachings created the foundation of the Seven Virtues of Rule, later adopted by the realm of Thassilon. While those seven virtues held aspects of charity, humility, and love, Lissala encouraged her followers to focus more prominently on teachings of duty and strict adherence to her guidelines. She believed, and taught, that those who followed her teachings would be greatly rewarded.

Followers of Lissala were encouraged to openly share knowledge and never keep secrets. Their ideology was based on the idea that knowledge is free, and it was their duty to share it as widely as possible. Often staunch rule followers, those who served The Order of Virtue found little gray in a world of absolutes. Those who took to a life of adventuring generally did so at the behest of another leader. They found comfort in being led and following someone with a strict sense of duty and structure.

These ideals were often misunderstood in the time of ancient Azlant, and many of Lissala’s teachings were twisted and corrupted as her message spread beyond Azlant and Thassilon. Eventually, she embraced the corruption herself. In modern worship, her true followers are few and she is rumored to have died. Rune giants refuse to accept this rumor and still worship her.

<column gap="tiny">

**Edicts** Gather and share knowledge freely, honor your duty when your word is given, respect and carry out the law

**Anathema** Conceal and keep information secret, break an oath, subvert authority

**Area of Concern**
Duty, fate, rewards for service, runes

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Myr](/Deities.aspx?ID=408)
(The Guide of Perfection)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 300

Myr, the Guide of Perfection, believed in honoring the past and respecting the wishes of the ancestors to follow an individual path forward. She taught that doing good deeds and living a charitable life honors those that came before and promotes a value in all life. While she promoted perfecting one’s body, it wasn’t her most significant edict. As society in Azlant prospered and grew, however, some took Myr’s teachings at face value. They prayed to Myr for aid in physical perfection and ignored the values of her other teachings. This made Myr an unconventional, but popular, goddess for warriors and athletes.

The traditional followers of Myr, while still caring for the physical bodies, resonate more closely with her teachings of charity. They will often be found performing acts of service for those less fortunate. Priests of Myr teach that if those with excess would share willingly, there will always be enough for all.

Marriage and family are very important to those who follow Myr. Honoring the past to continue the prosperity of the present and future is a key tenant among Myr’s followers, even for those who never had children themselves. Her following declined after the fall of the Azlanti empire, and those who remain can likely trace their lineage back to Myr’s first followers. Because of the importance of heritage to Myr and her followers, the fall from grace of her daughter [Sicva](/Deities.aspx?ID=411) is Myr’s greatest shame.

<column gap="tiny">

**Category** [Azlanti Pantheon](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=39)

**Edicts** Strive for perfection in mind and body, respect and honor those who came before you, use your means to aid the less fortunate

**Anathema** Take life needlessly, take from those less fortunate, ignore someone in need

**Area of Concern**
Charity, lineage, physical perfection

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Onos](/Deities.aspx?ID=409)
(God of Many Doors)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 302

Onos was the Azlanti deity of elements, embassies, and extraplanar travel. Often difficult for mortals to fully understand, the God of Many Doors exists outside of any personality expected by a deity and speaks with its followers in direct and emotionless ways. Priests of Onos do their best to behave in a similar manner. Slow to anger and mild-mannered, priests of Onos often served as mediators in times of conflict.

Onos is one of the few Azlanti gods to have a following that branches far beyond the Azlanti empire. As god of embassies and elements, Onos has several temples and places of worship within the Elemental Planes. These temples often serve as places of hospitality for planar travelers in locations that are less than hospitable.

As a deity of the elements, the people of ancient Azlant believed Onos to be one with the four elements. Onos, however, was always aware of the additional elemental planes of metal and wood. After its disciples began to spread the word of these additional elements, belief and worship of Onos decreased significantly. The people of the Azlanti empire believed in only four elemental planes and found it difficult to change their view. With the recent discovery of the two additional planes of wood and metal, some people in Golarion are returning to a faith in Onos in the hopes of gaining more knowledge about these oft-ignored planes.

<column gap="tiny">

**Category** [Azlanti Pantheon](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=39)

**Edicts** Mediate conflicts, respect each element equally, experience travel as often as possible

**Anathema** Act before weighing both sides, solve problems with aggression before negotiating, play favorites in negotiation

**Area of Concern**
Elements, embassies, extraplanar travel

</column>

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Scal](/Deities.aspx?ID=410)
(Calm after the Storm)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 302

The people of ancient Azlant worshipped Scal as the deity of catharsis and purity. His promotion of these values, however, was more violent in nature. The Calm After the Storm believed righteousness could only be found by harnessing one’s own darkness to destroy anything that threatened one’s existence. His teachings were not about suppressing one’s inner rage, hiding it, or blocking it out, but rather using that inherent violence as a powerful weapon.

A vengeful god, Scal once threatened to bring about an apocalypse by raining fire and destruction from the heavens onto all Golarion if humanity disappointed him. With these threats, he was wrongfully accused of causing Earthfall and the demise of ancient Azlant. His followers abandoned him, and Scal, scorned, left [Golarion](/Rules.aspx?ID=3298).

In recent years, reports of followers of Scal have been popping up around fight clubs, warrior guilds and similar groups founded through similar violent interests. Although small, these clusters of followers could indicate that Scal has returned to Golarion. To what end remains to be seen, however.

Often serving in underground arenas rather than peaceful temples, followers of Scal balk at traditional religious practice. Followers are often found among those cast out from families, loners, and recently released prisoners. These followers find refuge in Scal, as a god who doesn’t judge them for their inner rage, but rather one that encourages them to release it and use it as a source of power.

<column gap="tiny">

**Category** [Azlanti Pantheon](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=39)

**Edicts** Strive for physical and mental perfection, tap into one’s inner darkness to destroy anything that compromises one’s pursuit of righteous perfection, take vengeance when necessary

**Anathema** Indulge in gluttonous or lazy behavior, give up without a fight, refuse a duel

**Area of Concern**
Annihilation, catharsis, purity

**Pantheons**

</column>

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Sicva](/Deities.aspx?ID=411)
(Mistress of All)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 302

Sicva, the Mistress of All, was one of the most vile and destructive gods of ancient Azlanti. Despite being raised by her virtuous parents, the deities [Elion](/Deities.aspx?ID=405) and [Myr](/Deities.aspx?ID=408), Sicva grew up resentful of her father’s shifting nature and her mother’s commitment to charity. She set out to find ways to corrupt goodness and use that corruption for domination. She twisted her father’s ability to see all sides of an argument into an affinity for manipulation of the mind, and her mother’s penchant for charitable actions into an ability to oppress those of lesser means with a sickening smile. Sicva’s cruelty doesn’t stop at dominating civilized societies. She charges her followers to spread invasive species by bringing nonnative animals, plants, and viruses into new locations to destroy and corrupt precious ecosystems.

The Mistress of All is a popular deity of corrupt, power-hungry politicians and those with simple wills drawn to a domineering dogma steeped in degradation, manipulation and lies. The common people of Azlant who worshipped Sicva were often drawn in by lofty promises of wealth and fortune being theirs, if they were willing to go out and take it. Her followers typically dress in monochromatic clothes in tones of white, gray or black, reminiscent of military uniforms.

In modern times, worship of Sicva has dwindled, though small sects of followers could be found in societies with tyrannical and otherwise oppressive leadership.

<column gap="tiny">

**Category** [Azlanti Pantheon](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=39)

**Edicts** Spread invasive species to new ecosystems, corrupt good ideals to promote domination and oppression, inspire duty and loyalty through coercion and fear

**Anathema** Support a rebellion, give freely expecting nothing in return, aid in ending a plague or infection

**Area of Concern**
Domination, invasive species, oppression

</column>
